reasoning: The finding is technically real but overstated in severity. Confirmed code path: AjaxHandler.php lines 74-77 construct the nonce action string as `'wpdesk_notice_dismiss_' . sanitize_text_field($_POST['notice_name'])`, then pass this attacker-controlled string directly to `check_ajax_referer($optionName, ...)`. WordPress nonces are symmetric: wp_create_nonce(X) produces a token that only validates with check_ajax_referer(X). Because the action string fed to check_ajax_referer is derived from POST input, any authenticated user can call wp_create_nonce('wpdesk_notice_dismiss_<anything>') to pre-generate a valid nonce for any action name, then supply the matching notice_name — the referer check passes trivially. This defeats the binding of the nonce to a server-defined specific operation. Reachability is confirmed: hook is wp_ajax_ (login required, line 42), and there is an additional current_user_can('edit_posts') gate at line 78. The nonce is generated by PermanentDismissibleNotice.php:45 as `wp_create_nonce($this->noticeDismissOptionName)` and embedded in the page HTML — so legitimate callers also receive the nonce via the rendered page. The CSRF protection is genuinely bypassable: an attacker logged in as an editor-level user (edit_posts) can directly call this endpoint for any notice_name without needing a CSRF-delivered nonce, since they can self-generate one. However, the severity should be LOW rather than MEDIUM because the only write consequence is `update_option('wpdesk_notice_dismiss_<name>', '1')` — marking a notice as dismissed. There is no privilege escalation, data exfiltration, or significant harm. The do_action('wpdesk_notice_dismissed_notice', ...) hook is a theoretical secondary concern but has no harmful default handlers in this plugin. Downgrading from medium to low on impact grounds.
